coredata.dc:title: Thermal polymerization of ion-modified carbon dots into multi-functional LiF-carbon interface for stabilizing SiO anode
coredata.dc:creator: Qian, Yong
Liang, Yazhan
Zhang, Weiqing
Xi, Baojuan
Lin, Ning
coredata.dc:description: SiO has been demonstrated as one type of promising high-capacity anode for Li-ion batteries. However, the parasitic reactions between the electrolyte and SiO interface, severer than that of the well-developed graphite, are generally overlook. Herein, a multi-functional LiF-carbon co-coating layer on SiO (SiO@LCD) is designed to resist the interfacial reactions, which originates from thermal-assembly of zero-dimensional Li-absorbed and F-doped carbon dots produced by hydro/alcohol-thermal reaction of discarded wood sawdust with a high yield of 96.9 %. Combined with advanced spectroscopy characterizations and theoretical calculations, the LiF-carbon co-coating layer has been determined to induce preferential decomposition of LiPF6 to form a thin and dense solid electrolyte interface (SEI) with LiF-rich inner layer, and promote the rapid transmission of ion/electron. As a result, the initial Coulombic efficiency (CE) of the SiO@LCD increases from 66.1 % to 78.6 %, and the average CE is increased by 0.3 % at 0.2 A g−1 with enhanced rate capability and cycling stability during the (de)lithiation process. More importantly, the assembled 3.7 Ah pouch cell exhibits a long-term cycling life over 400 cycles at 0.2 C with the capacity retention of 85.1 %.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Li-absorbed and F-doped carbon dots
Multi-functional coating layer
Preferential decomposition of LiPF6
Enhanced ion/electron transport
LiF-rich interface film

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22075268 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration for the fabrication of F-CD-Li and SiO@LCD. (b) PL spectra (excitation at 365 nm) of as-synthesized F-CD-Li solution (inset images are the digital photos of original F-CD-Li solution and excited F-CD-Li solution, respectively). (c) The yield of carbon dots at different solvents. (d) The high-resolution F 1 s and Li 1 s spectra of F-CD-Li. (e) The SEM images of original SiO sheet (left) and carbonized SiO sheet coated with carbon dots (right), respectively. The SEM images of (f) SiO, (g) SiO@CD and (h) SiO@LCD. (i) The HRTEM image of SiO@LCD. (j) XRD patterns and (k) Raman spectra of SiO, SiO@CD and SiO@LCD. (l) The high-resolution F 1 s and Li 1 s spectra of SiO@LCD, respectively (The content of F is about 0.7 at. %).

(a) The first charge/discharge curves and (b) the cycling performance of SiO, SiO@CD and SiO@LCD electrodes at 0.2 A g−1. (c) The rate capabilities at various current rates of SiO, SiO@CD and SiO@LCD electrodes. The diffusion coefficients of lithium ions during the (d) discharge and (e) charge process calculated from the GITT curves as a function of cell voltage for SiO, SiO@CD and SiO@LCD electrodes at 0.1 A g−1. (f) Contribution ratios of the adsorption capacity at different scan rates of SiO, SiO@CD and SiO@LCD electrodes. (g) The DC polarization curves of SiO and SiO@LCD electrodes at 0.005 V. The DRT transformation of in-situ EIS spectra for (h) SiO@LCD and (i) SiO electrodes.

The high-resolution F 1 s, O 1 s and Si 2p spectra along the sputtering time of (a) SiO@LCD and (b) SiO electrodes after 10 cycles at 0.2 A g−1. (c) The 3D visualization of the architectural evolutions of LiCO3 − and SiO2 −, (d) the 2D visualization of the surface distribution of C−, O− and F− and (e) the digital images and SEM images for SiO@LCD (up) and SiO (down) electrodes after 10 cycles at 0.2 A g−1. (f) Schematic illustration for the influence of F-CD-Li coating on the electrochemical performance of SiO anode.

DFT calculations for lithium ion migrate in (a) LiF/C, (b) LiF and (c) C models. (d) The calculated migration energy barrier of lithium ion diffusion in the LiF/C, LiF and C models. (e) The adsorption energy of LiPF6, FEC, DEC and EC molecules on the LiF/C, LiF and C models, respectively. (f) Schematic illustration for the action mechanism of LiF-carbon layer.

The electrochemical performance of the blended SiO and graphite composites with the mass ratio of 1:9. (a) The first charge/discharge curves and (b) the cycling performance of the Gr, Gr+SiO and Gr+SiO@LCD electrodes at 0.1 A g−1. (c) The rate performance of the Gr, Gr+SiO and Gr+SiO@LCD electrodes. (d) The charge/discharge curves of the Gr+SiO@LCD electrode at different current densities. (e) The cycling performance of the Gr, Gr+SiO and Gr+SiO@LCD electrodes at 0.5 A g−1. The electrochemical performance of pouch cell with the blended SiO@LCD/Gr anode (Note that charge process is constant-current constant-voltage (CCCV) to 4.45 V, then constant-voltage (CV) to 0.02 C). (f) The rate performance of pouch cell and (g) the corresponding charge/discharge curves at different current densities. (h) The cycling performance of pouch cell at 0.2 C (1 C = 3700 mA g−1) (Inset digital image is the as-assembled pouch cell).

head.ce:title: Thermal polymerization of ion-modified carbon dots into multi-functional LiF-carbon interface for stabilizing SiO anode

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yongjiang Laboratory, Ningbo, Zhejiang 315202, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aYongjiang Laboratory, Ningbo, Zhejiang 315202, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Synchrotron Radiation Laboratory, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, Anhui 230026, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bNational Synchrotron Radiation Laboratory, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, Anhui 230026, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University, Jinan, Shandong 250100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cSchool of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University, Jinan, Shandong 250100, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: SiO has been demonstrated as one type of promising high-capacity anode for Li-ion batteries. However, the parasitic reactions between the electrolyte and SiO interface, severer than that of the well-developed graphite, are generally overlook. Herein, a multi-functional LiF-carbon co-coating layer on SiO (SiO@LCD) is designed to resist the interfacial reactions, which originates from thermal-assembly of zero-dimensional Li-absorbed and F-doped carbon dots produced by hydro/alcohol-thermal reaction of discarded wood sawdust with a high yield of 96.9 %. Combined with advanced spectroscopy characterizations and theoretical calculations, the LiF-carbon co-coating layer has been determined to induce preferential decomposition of LiPF6 to form a thin and dense solid electrolyte interface (SEI) with LiF-rich inner layer, and promote the rapid transmission of ion/electron. As a result, the initial Coulombic efficiency (CE) of the SiO@LCD increases from 66.1 % to 78.6 %, and the average CE is increased by 0.3 % at 0.2 A g−1 with enhanced rate capability and cycling stability during the (de)lithiation process. More importantly, the assembled 3.7 Ah pouch cell exhibits a long-term cycling life over 400 cycles at 0.2 C with the capacity retention of 85.1 %.

A uniform and dense LiF-carbon co-coating layer on SiO anode is fabricated by thermal polymerization of zero-dimensional Li-absorbed and F-doped carbon dots, which can induce preferential decomposition of LiPF6 to form a thin and dense solid electrolyte interface (SEI) with LiF-rich inner layer and promote the ion/electron transport, thereby improving the Coulombic efficiency, rate and cycling performance of SiO electrode.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Silicon monoxide (SiO) has been demonstrated as a promising high-capacity anode material for developing high-energy density rechargeable lithium ion batteries (LIBs) in the ever-growing field of portable electronics and electric vehicles [1,2]. The electrochemical lithiation reaction of SiO can be formulated as SiO + Li+ + e− → LixSi + LiySiOz (or + Li2O), which endows SiO anode with a higher theoretical lithiation capacity about 2600 mA h g−1 than that of graphite (372 mA h g−1) [3,4]. Based on above electrochemical reaction, the generated LixSi alloy contributes to the reversible capacity in the subsequent cycles [5,6]. The electrochemically inert LiySiOz product could be served as buffer component to accommodate volume change of LixSi alloy, making SiO exhibits a more stable cycling life than that of a silicon anode [7–9].

However, one of the main challenges of SiO anode is the unsatisfactory initial Coulombic efficiency (ICE) caused by the formation of irreversible LiySiOz and ultralow intrinsic conductivity [10]. Thus, excess cathode materials are required to compensate the consumption of lithium ion in full cells, which is adverse for improving the actual energy density of the LIBs [11]. To address this issue, various strategies such as carbon coating, pre-lithiation, and pre-magnesiation have been proposed, and coating carbon layer has been demonstrated as an effective and low-cost method to improve the electrochemical performance [2,4,12,13]. For example, Hou group used humic acid as a feedstock to synthesize in situ graphene-coated SiO anode, exhibiting an initial charge capacity of 1515.2 mA h g−1 with ICE of 78.2 % at 0.1 A g−1 [14].

On the other hand, an often overlooked but detrimental obstacle faced by SiO and all Si-based anode materials is the continue parasitic reactions with electrolyte [15]. It is because that the repeated non-negligible volume change (∼200 %) would cause unstable solid electrolyte interface (SEI) film, and the highly active lithiated product LixSi could spontaneously react with electrolyte [16,17]. Exploiting suitable electrolyte additives to promote the formation of mechanical and chemical stable SEI film or developing new binders with rigid/flexible composite framework and self-healing effect to accommodate large volume expansion have been shown to inhabit parasitic reactions [18]. For instance, Cui group combined fluoroethylene carbonate and 1,3-propanediolcyclic sulfate as hybrid electrolyte additives to induce the formation of SEI film containing LiF, inorganic sulfate and organic sulfate on the surface of SiOx@C particles, contributing to excellent ionic conductivity and suppressing the electrolyte decomposition [19]. Recently, constructing an electrochemical inert layer on SiO-based anode materials to replace pure carbon layer has also determined to suppress the side reactions. For example, Wei et al. introduced an anatase-phase TiO2 layer to construct SiOx@TiO2@C composite, so as to achieve a highly stable interface and decreased resistance to electron and ion diffusion [20]. Therefore, combining carbonaceous and inorganic coating layer is of great significant to further optimize the electrochemical performance of SiO-based anode materials.

Carbon dots (CDs) have sparked a research climax because of their quantum size (less than 10 nm), abundant surface functional groups, uniform dispersion, and tunable structure and composition [21]. CDs and their derivatives also demonstrate extensive application in energy related fields, such as lithium/sodium-ion batteries and supercapacitors [22–24]. Herein, taking advantages of the heteroatoms-rich and absorption properties, a type of Li-absorbed and F-doped CDs is produced by hydro/alcohol-thermal reaction of discarded Balsa wood sawdust with a high yield of 96.9 %, which are then adsorbed on micro-sized SiO particles. After annealing treatment, the zero-dimentional CDs undergo self-assembly and carbonization process, forming a LiF-modified carbonaceous layer coated on SiO particles (SiO@LCD). The various in-situ tests and density functional theory (DFT) calculations exhibit that the LiF-carbon layer on the surface of SiO particles can promote the transmission of ion/electron and induce the pre-decomposition of LiPF6 to form a thin and uniform SEI with inner layer rich in LiF, which together promote the significant improvement of Coulombic efficiency, cycling life and rate capability. As a result, the SiO@LCD electrode exhibits better electrochemical performance than that of the SiO and LiF-free carbon-coated SiO electrodes with higher average Coulombic efficiency and better cycling stability. The as-assembled Ah-level pouch cell displays exciting cycling stability with a capacity of 3105.4 mA h after 400 cycles at 0.2 C and a capacity retention of 85.1 %.

Natural abundant biomass is an ideal precursor for low-cost carbonaceous materials with tunable micro-nano structure, dimension, porosity, graphitization degree and specific surface area [25]. With that in mind, the zero-dimensional Li-adsorbed and F-doped carbon dots (denoted as F-CD-Li) were produced by hydro/alcohol-thermal treatment of discarded Balsa wood sawdust, which are then employed to composite with microsized SiO particles, forming LiF-carbon co-coated SiO (denoted as SiO@LCD) particles after calcination, as schematically illustrated in Fig. 1 a. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra excited at 365 nm of the as-synthesized F-CD-Li solution exhibits an obvious peak at about 455 nm, demonstrating a strong fluorescence effect, which is consistent with the corresponding digital images (Fig. 1b) [26]. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) image show that the size of F-CD-Li is about 3.0 nm, further demonstrating that are carbon dots (Fig. S1). Moreover, the solvent for solvothermal reaction is optimized as a mixture of water and ethanol (volume ratio of 1:1) for a high yield of 96.9 % with water-soluble lithium bis(trifluoromethane sulfonimide) (LiTFSI) as Li and F source (Fig. 1c). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement further displays that F element in the F-CD-Li samples mainly exists in the form of C-F, while Li-OH for Li element, which demonstrates that F is doped with CDs and Li is mainly adsorbed on the surface of CDs (Figs. 1d and S2) [27–29].

Based on the strong adsorption of CDs and the ability to self-assemble from zero-dimensional to 2D structure during heating, the F-CD-Li are considered as carbon source to coat SiO particles [30]. As a proof of concept, a SiO sheet formed by polishing is first used as the substrate to adsorb a certain amount of F-CD-Li solution and then heat treated. Obviously, the F-CD-Li transforms into 2D carbon layer on the SiO sheet, smooth and flat (Fig. 1e). This situation has also been further verified on the surface of SiO particles, whether coated with CDs or F-CD-Li, the original rough surface of SiO particles becomes very smooth, indicating excellent carbon coating (Figs. 1f-1h and S3). The thickness of carbon layer for SiO@LCD and CDs-coated SiO (denoted as SiO@CD) revealed by high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) are about 7.6 nm and 11.0 nm, respectively (Figs. 1i, S4 and S5). Obviously, the carbon layer of the SiO@LCD sample is distributed with LiF nanoparticles with a size less than 2 nm. The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) curves indicate that the carbon content is about 2.8 wt. % and 3.2 wt. % for SiO@LCD and SiO@CD, respectively (Fig. S6). Besides, it can be observed that the SiO matrix does not exhibit obvious disproportionation after carbon coating, and still shows disordered structure. The scanning TEM (STEM) and the corresponding elemental mapping verify that F, C, O, Si elements in SiO@LCD are all uniformly distributed, so are elements in SiO and SiO@CD samples (Figs. S7–S9).

To further investigate the evolution of the microstructures of SiO@LCD and SiO@CD, a series of measurements such as X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Raman are performed. Fig. 1j shows the XRD patterns of SiO@LCD, SiO@CD and SiO, the result indicates that the characteristic peaks of Si in SiO matrix are only slightly enhanced after carbon coating, suggesting insignificant disproportionation reaction, which is in accord with the HRTEM tests. The Raman spectra of SiO@LCD, SiO@CD and SiO all exhibit similar broad peaks at about 158.0, 317.9 and 493.5 cm−1, belonging to the TA model, LA&LO model and TO model of amorphous Si in SiO matrix, respectively (Fig. 1k) [31]. The results further demonstrate that carbon coating does not disproportionate the original SiO matrix to generate a large number of crystalline substances. Moreover, it should be pointed out that peak located at 520.7 cm−1 which can be attributed to crystalline Si is significantly weakened after carbon coating and even disappears in the SiO@LCD sample, indicating that the carbon layer is uniform and dense. The ratios of IG (located at about 1600 cm−1, representing crystalline graphite in the carbon matrix) and ID (located at about 1350 cm−1, assigning to the defect in the carbon matrix) are also calculated as 0.47 and 0.41 for SiO@LCD and SiO@CD, respectively (Fig. S10) [32]. So the F-CD-Li as carbon source can obtain a carbon layer with higher degree of graphitization than that of pure CDs. In addition, SiO@LCD, SiO@CD and SiO samples exhibit the similar type IV isotherms with the special surface area of 8.81, 13.60 and 1.68 m2 g−1, respectively (Fig. S11) [33]. The results reveal that the carbon layer originated from carbon dots is very dense with fewer pores. The XPS test is also carried out to explore the surface chemical configuration of SiO@LCD and SiO@CD. Obviously, the high-resolution F 1 s and Li 1 s spectra exhibit that LiF is formed on the surface of SiO@LCD (Fig. 1l) [34]. Moreover, the SiO@LCD sample shows a higher proportion of C=C/CC bonds compared to SiO@CD, manifesting less surface oxygen-containing functional groups, in accordance with the results of Raman (Fig. S12) [35]. And the high-resolution Si 2p spectra indicates that most of Si0 is transformed into higher valence state such as Si4+ after carbon coating, which can also act as a protective layer to prevent it from interacting with electrolyte during the electrochemical cycle (Fig. S13) [15]. Above all, the zero-dimensional Li-adsorbed and F-doped carbon dots as carbon source to coat SiO can obtain a uniform and dense LiF-carbon layer with high degree of graphitization and few defects.

To evaluate the electrochemical Li-storage performance of SiO@LCD and SiO@CD samples, a series of measurements are executed using half coins with metallic lithium foil as counter/reference electrode. The influence of calcination temperature and carbon dot content on the electrochemical performance of SiO electrode is first explored and showed in Fig. S14. Fig. 2 a exhibits the first charge/discharge curves of SiO@LCD, SiO@CD and SiO electrodes at 0.2 A g−1. Obviously, the SiO@LCD electrode exhibits a high charge capacity of 1751.3 mA h g−1 with initial Coulombic efficiency (ICE) of 78.6 %, and a capacity of 1636.9 mA h g−1 with ICE of 70.7 % for SiO@CD electrode, 1593.7 mA h g−1 with ICE of 66.1 % for SiO electrode. After 300 cycles, the SiO@LCD electrode still displays a high capacity of 804.8 mA h g−1 with the average Coulombic efficiency (CE) of 99.6 % (Figs. 2b and S15). As a contrast, the SiO electrode only show a capacity of 96.8 mA h g−1 after 300 cycles with the average CE of 99.3 %, and a capacity of 250.1 mA h g−1 for SiO@C with the average CE of 99.3 %. Notably, a high CE of 93.5 % can be obtained at the second cycle for SiO@LCD electrode, while 84.9 % for SiO@CD and 81.5 % for SiO, indicating more stable solid electrolyte interface (SEI) film. The results display that LiF-carbon co-coating can significantly improve the cycling stability of SiO electrode and inhibit the side reaction between electrode and electrolyte. And it should be pointed out that the better cycling stability can be further achieved by optimizing the electrolyte, adding single-walled CNTs and using new binders [36–38]. The rate performance of SiO@LCD, SiO@CD and SiO electrodes are also evaluated in Figs. 2c and S16. The SiO@LCD electrodes can exhibit a high capacity of 546.6 mA h g−1 at 5.0 A g−1, while only a capacity of 221.2 mA h g−1 for SiO@CD and a capacity of 14.3 mA h g−1 for SiO, implying excellent rate capability. Moreover, the SiO@LCD electrode also exhibits better electrochemical performance compared to the other reports SiO/C materials in term of ICE, rate capability and cycling stability (Table S1) [2,12,39-41].

The reaction kinetics of SiO@LCD, SiO@CD and SiO electrodes is further investigated by galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT) and in-situ electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements. It is obvious that the SiO@LCD electrode exhibits the highest lithium ion diffusion coefficient with the smallest Ohmic polarization among all electrodes in both charging and discharge processes, demonstrating LiF-carbon co-coating contributes to increase the migration rate of lithium ions and electrons (Figs. 2d, 2e and S17) [42]. Besides, the scanning cyclic voltammetry (CV) test show that carbon coating can enhance its capacitive behavior of SiO electrode with higher capacitive contribution ratio at different scan rates (Figs. 2f, S18 and S19) [43]. However, it is worth noting that the capacitive contribution ratio of the SiO@LCD electrode is lower than that of the SiO@CD electrode, further demonstrating its LiF-carbon layer has fewer defects. Moreover, the direct current (DC) polarization curve of the SiO@LCD electrode displays the lowest leakage current at 0.005 V and quickly reaches a stable state, further signifying that LiF-carbon can be used as an excellent protective layer to obtain stable SEI film (Fig. 2g). In order to more clearly explore the evolution of the kinetic parameters of the electrodes during the cycle, in-situ EIS measurement is also performed (Fig. S20). Markedly, the SiO@LCD electrode exhibits a smaller impedance during the electrochemical cycle than that of SiO electrode, which is consistent with the rate test. The distribution of relaxation time (DRT) concept has acted as promising techniques because it can directly distinguish the time constant of electrochemical process from EIS data and avoid the fitting error [44]. Accordingly, the DRT function calculated by the MATLAB CUI tool box developed by Ciucci's group to show the dynamics information more clearly (Figs. 2h, 2i and S21) [45]. Based on the previous reports, the peak above 10 s belongs to the lithium ion diffusion into bulk, the peak located at 10−2–10 s can be assigned to the charge transfer between bulk and surface layer, and the peak below 10−2 s originates from the interface reaction at SEI [46]. So, it is noticeable that the SiO@LCD electrode displays a more stable and smaller R SEI than that of SiO electrode. Besides, the SiO@LCD electrode exhibits significantly smaller R ct and R D at the initial stage of discharge, demonstrating fast electron/ion transport. And when discharged to 0.005 V, the peak values for R ct and R D of the SiO@LCD electrode are about 16 and 77 Ω, while those of the SiO electrode are about 81 and 95 Ω, respectively. During the charge process, the R ct and R D of both electrodes gradually increase. More importantly, it should be noted that the SiO@LCD electrode exhibits a smaller R ct with a larger R D at the end of charging, demonstrating better cycle reversibility. Therefore, combined with electrochemical performance and kinetic characterizations, it can be concluded that LiF-carbon co-coating formed by F-CD-Li as a carbon source can not only significantly improve the cycling stability of SiO electrode with inhibition of the side reaction between the electrolyte and electrode during the cycle, but also enhance the reaction kinetics of electrode with acceleration of electron/ion transport.

The evolution of SEI film on the SiO@LCD electrode is also evaluated to reveal the advantages of LiF-carbon layer characterized by sputtering XPS and time of flight secondary ion mass spectrometry (TOF-SIMS). Figs. 3 a, 3b, S22 and S23 show the variation trend of the surface element and surface chemical configuration with sputtering time. It could be found that the surface elements of the SiO@LCD electrode show a smooth change, indicating a uniform SEI. Furthermore, the surface of the SiO@LCD electrode is mainly composed of LiF, Li2CO3, Li2SiO3 and Li4SiO4, while LixPFy, LiF, Li2CO3, ROLi, Li2SiO3 and Li4SiO4 for the SiO electrode [47]. For high-resolution F 1 s spectra, the SiO@LCD electrode only shows LiF after sputtering 200 s, in contrast, LixPFy and LiF can be detected on the surface of the SiO electrode, demonstrating the SEI generated on the LiF-carbon layer is denser [48]. For high-resolution Si 2p spectra, the signals of some Si and LixSi alloy can be found in a short sputtering time, manifesting that its SEI is very thin [49]. To sum up, LiF-carbon layer on the surface of the SiO@LCD electrode can induce the electrolyte to produce a thin, uniform and dense LiF-rich SEI film. The HRTEM images also reveal that thickness of the SiO@LCD and SiO electrodes are about 2.1 nm and 11.9 nm after 10 cycles at 0.2 A g−1, respectively, in accordance with the sputtering XPS results (Fig. S24).

The TOF-SIMS measurement is applied to visualize the evolution and distribution of electrode surface components. The depth profiling curves of TOF-SIMS shows that the SiO@LCD electrode has a higher content of F− but with lower content of LiO−, LiCO3 −, SiO2 −, C− and O− than that of the SiO electrode (Fig. S25). Besides, the content of LiO−, LiCO3 −, SiO2 − and C− for the SiO@LCD electrode quickly reach a stable state with the increasing sputtering time. These results further demonstrate that the SEI formed on the surface of the SiO@LCD electrode is rich in LiF and has the characteristics of thin, uniform and dense. The 3D visualization of the architectural evolutions of LiCO3 −, SiO2 −, C−, O−, F− and LiO− also illustrate this point more concretely (Figs. 3c and S26). Moreover, the 2D visualization of the surface distribution of C−, O−, F−, LiO−, LiCO3 − and SiO2 − further suggests that the SiO@LCD electrode has a more uniform SEI film induced by LiF-carbon layer (Figs. 3d and S27).

In order to explore the effect of LiF-carbon layer on the SiO electrode from the electrode scale, the electrodes after cycling are also detected. Surprisingly, most of the active materials on the copper foil of the SiO electrode falls off after cycling, losing electrical contact, which may also be one of the reasons for its rapid capacity decay (Fig. 3e). In sharp contrast, the SiO@LCD electrode still exhibits a complete structure without active materials shedding. The SEM images further show that the SiO@LCD particles remain almost unchanged after cycling, unlike the SiO particles which are completely crushed, demonstrating the LiF-carbon layer can also inhibit the breakage of the SiO particles during the electrochemical cycle, thereby improving its cycling stability (Figs. S28 and S29). The atomic force microscope (AFM) tests for the SiO@LCD and SiO electrodes after cycling are also carried out to detect the potential distribution on the surface of the electrode, which can intuitively reflect the conductivity and uniformity of the electrode. Obviously, on the one hand, the original SiO@LCD electrode exhibits a smaller surface potential, and on the other hand, the surface potential is increased after cycling but is still less than the SiO electrode, demonstrating the LiF-carbon layer can not only enhance the overall conductivity of SiO, but also restrain the decomposition of the electrolyte, so that the electrode has less internal resistance after cycling (Figs. S30 and S31). Moreover, the SiO@LCD electrode shows a narrow potential distribution, which also expresses a more uniform SEI film. Therefore, LiF-carbon co-coating can induce electrolyte decomposition to produce a dense, uniform and thin SEI film rich in LiF, and inhibit the volume expansion of the electrode during cycling, which together promote the SiO@LCD electrode to exhibit significantly improved cycling stability and cycle CE, and the relevant schematics are described in Fig. 3f.

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations are also carried out to further elucidate the effect of LiF-carbon layer on electrochemical performance. Firstly, three models, LiF/C, LiF and C, are established to investigate the migration of lithium ions, which is closely related to the dynamic capabilities (Fig. 4 a–4c). Obviously, lithium ion exhibits the smallest migrate energy barrier in LiF model, and when LiF is combined with carbon, the diffusion energy barrier of lithium ion increases slightly, but it is still significantly smaller than its diffusion in C model (Fig. 4d). These results indicate that the addition of LiF can contribute to improve the ion transport ability of the carbon layer. Besides, based on Raman characterization, the combination of LiF and carbon helps to increase the degree of graphitization of carbon, which will promote the electron transport of the overall coating layer. Thus, it can be considered that the LiF-carbon layer can significantly promote the transport of ion/electron, contributing to excellent rate performance. The adsorption energy of each component of the electrolyte on the surface of each model surface is also calculated, so this can represent the reaction trend to some extent. Surprisingly, the adsorption energy of LiPF6 in models containing LiF is much higher than that of the other organic components such as EC, DEC and FEC molecules, indicating that the combination of LiF will induce the preferential adsorption of LiPF6 (Figs. 4e and S32). In this way, the preferentially adsorbed LiPF6 will be preferentially decomposed in electrochemical reactions, leading to the final generation of the inner layer of the SEI film rich in inorganic layers such as LiF, which is consistent with sputtering XPS and TOF-SIMS results. Therefore, combined with experiments and theory analysis, it can be concluded that the construction of LiF-carbon layer can not only promote the rapid transmission of ion/electron, but also preferentially induce the decomposition of LiPF6 to produce a thin and uniform SEI with inner layer rich in LiF, thereby inhibiting the subsequent decomposition of the electrolyte, which comprehensively improves the rate and cycle performance of the battery (Fig. 4f).

To further evaluate the practicality of the SiO@LCD sample, the blended SiO@LCD and graphite (Gr) composites at a mass ratio of 1:9 are also tested with coin cells and pouch cells. Obviously, the Gr+SiO@LCD electrode exhibits a high charge capacity of 540.4 mA h g−1 with the ICE of 84.4 % at 0.1 A g−1 (Fig. 5 a). In contrast, the Gr+SiO electrode deliver a capacity of 511.9 mA h g−1 with the ICE of 80.5 %, and the Gr electrode show a capacity of 367.8 mA h g−1 with the ICE of 86.7 %. The results not only indicate that the addition of SiO@LCD can significantly increase the specific capacity of the Gr anode, but also demonstrate that the SiO@LCD can improve the ICE of the anode compared with SiO, which all contribute to enhance the energy density of batteries. After 200 cycles, the Gr+SiO@LCD electrode still exhibits a high capacity of 451.3 mA h g−1 with the capacity retention of 83.5 %, while 307.6 mA h g−1 with the capacity retention of 60.0 % for the SiO electrode, indicating good cycling stability (Fig. 5b). Moreover, the rate performance of the Gr+SiO@LCD electrode at different current densities are also estimated (Fig. 5c). The Gr+SiO@LCD electrode delivers the capacities of 559.9, 523.4, 456.5, 444.1, 407.6 and 372.8 mA h g−1 at 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 0.6, 0.8 and 1.0 A g−1, respectively, which are higher than those of the SiO electrode at the same current density, suggesting fast ion transport. The related charge/discharge curves are shown in Figs. 5d and S33, consistent with the electrochemical performance of the corresponding SiO-based anodes. The Gr+SiO@LCD electrode also shows excellent cycling stability at a high current density of 0.5 A g−1, and displays a high capacity of 412.1 mA h g−1 after 200 cycles with almost no attenuation (Figs. 5e and S34). Comparatively, the Gr+SiO electrode only exhibits a capacity of 289.6 mA h g−1 after 200 cycles with the capacity retention of about 72.3 %, further implying that SiO@LCD has excellent structural stability.

Based on the excellent electrochemical performance of the Gr+SiO@LCD in coin cells, its application in pouch cells is also investigated with the commercialization standard. The assembled pouch cell has a designed capacity of 3700 mA h with a mass load of about 10.0 mg cm−2 for the Gr+SiO@LCD anode, about 25.0 mg cm−2 for the LiCoO2 cathode and the electrolyte holding capacity of about 1.35 g Ah−1. Evidently, the pouch cell exhibits capacities of 3710.3, 3649.6, 3515.9, 3406.3 and 3118.7 mA h at 0.2 C, 0.5 C, 1.0 C, 2.0 C and 3.0 C, respectively (Fig. 5f). Surprisingly, when the current density is increased to 4.0 C, the pouch cell can still show a high capacity of 2686.7 mA h, demonstrating great rate capability. Besides, a high capacity of 3659.7 mA h can be obtained as the current returns to 0.2 C under a series of high-current densities, manifesting good structural stability. The charge/discharge curves at different current rates show that the pouch cell exhibits a small voltage polarization even at 4.0 C (equivalent to 14.8 A g−1) (Fig. 5g). More importantly, the pouch cell displays exciting cycling stability with a capacity of 3105.4 mA h after 400 cycles at 0.2 C and a capacity retention of 85.1 % (Fig. 5h). Therefore, the SiO@LCD exhibits great application prospects with excellent cycling stability and rate performance whether in the coin cell or in the pouch cell, which further demonstrates that LiF-carbon can used as a good protective layer for SiO-based materials.

In summary, Li-absorbed and F-doped CDs is produced by hydro/alcohol-thermal reaction of discarded Balsa wood sawdust with a high yield of 96.9 %, which are then adsorbed on the SiO microparticles, forming a LiF-carbon layer after carbonization. Combined with the electrochemical measurements, various in-situ characterizations and DFT analysis, it is confirmed that the LiF-carbon layer on the surface of SiO particles can not only promote the fast transmission of ion/electron, but also induce the pre-decomposition of LiPF6 to produce a thin and uniform SEI with inner layer rich in LiF, thereby inhibiting the subsequent reduction of the electrolyte and restricting the fragmentation of SiO particles. Thus, the SiO@LCD electrodes exhibit excellent rate performance and significantly improved cycling stability with higher average CEs. The as-assembled Ah-level pouch cell exhibits excellent cycling stability with a capacity of 3105.4 mA h after 400 cycles at 0.2 C and a capacity retention of 85.1 %.

Yong Qian: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Yazhan Liang: Software, Resources. Weiqing Zhang: Formal analysis, Investigation. Baojuan Xi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources. Ning Lin: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22075268).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.ensm.2023.102996.
